items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: Which method of heat transfer cooks food in a microwave?
items.correct: Radiation — microwaves are electromagnetic radiation that makes polar water molecules vibrate, generating heat.
items.misconception: Microwaves cook food from the inside out.
items.why: Microwaves penetrate ~1–2 cm into food and excite water molecules — they cook outer regions first, conducting heat inward. The 'inside out' myth comes from feeling cool outer surfaces after microwaving (because steam carries heat out).

items.q: Can you only get good-quality protein from meat?
items.correct: No - eggs, dairy, fish, soya and quinoa are HBV proteins, and combining plant LBV proteins (e.g. beans + bread) supplies all essential amino acids.
items.misconception: Yes - meat is the only source of complete protein.
items.random_wrong: Protein is only found in drinks.
items.why: Students equate protein with meat. Soya and quinoa are plant HBV proteins, and protein complementation (rice + lentils, beans + toast) gives vegetarians the full set of essential amino acids without any meat.

items.q: Can vitamin C from fruit be lost before you eat it?
items.correct: Yes - vitamin C is water-soluble and destroyed by heat, light and oxygen, so boiling and long storage reduce it.
items.misconception: No - vitamins cannot be destroyed; cooked and raw fruit and veg are identical.
items.random_wrong: Vitamin C is only lost if food is frozen.
items.why: Students treat vitamin content as fixed. Vitamin C leaches into cooking water and breaks down with heat and oxidation - which is why steaming or microwaving veg briefly, and eating it fresh, preserves far more than long boiling.
Correct answer: Fat. “Carbohydrate” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's 30 g. “10 g” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 4 kcal. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
